title: The RTD Cocktail Industry Is Brutal. The ‘Summer House’ Drama Proves It
summary:
In the midst of scandal, cast member Kyle Cooke has found a lifeline for his RTD cocktail brand, Loverboy, after struggling to stay afloat.

The post The RTD Cocktail Industry Is Brutal. The ‘Summer House’ Drama Proves It appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
The Bravo drama that has captivated a nation is also indirectly shining a light on the state of the drinks business.

When Summer House costars Amanda Batula and West Wilson confirmed growing speculation and announced they were romantically involved in a joint Instagram Story statement on March 31, the internet let out a collective gasp. For years Batula, who is currently separated from her husband and fellow costar Kyle Cooke, had consoled her close friend Ciara Miller, who dated West in 2021 and still had strong feelings for him. Meanwhile, during the lowest points of Batula’s marriage, Ciara championed, supported, and encouraged her friend, telling her that she was strong enough to be on her own. 

As Batula and Wilson have seemingly doubled down on their romance, despite aggressive public backlash, a few clear winners have emerged. Ciara has dazzled on various red carpets as a correspondent. Cast members like Lindsay Hubbard and Carl Radke have parlayed heightened visibility into brand deals. And Cooke, who has overnight become a sympathetic figure, has found a lifeline for his RTD (ready-to-drink) brand, Loverboy. 

“I have an investor call tomorrow. We have to talk about how long we can last based on our current cash reserves,” Cooke had shared on an episode as recently as March 24. He said the brand had “six months at most.” He even confessed that Loverboy’s financial woes had led him to start DJ’ing “to pay the bills.” (Later in the episode, cast member Ben Waddell shared that Cooke “personally guaranteed a $4 million loan because Loverboy was doing well and it was going up. And there’s $2.1 million left on the loan.”)

You know things are serious when someone has to turn to DJing as a financial lifeline, but Cooke might soon be able to hang up his headphones. After capitalizing on his scandal-adjacent “Carl’s a Mess” meme to sell Loverboy merch, he shared on Instagram on April 8,  “Your support and your purchases over the last week have made a huge impact and are literally helping keep the lights on.” (Cooke did not respond to our requests for an interview.)

Now, on the Loverboy website, seemingly all of the beverages from the margaritas to limoncellos are either sold out or only available for preorder. 

Summer Should Be Fun. Crowded RTD Market? Not Fun

Given the RTD boom of the last decade, Loverboy’s pre-“Scamanda” financial woes are somewhat surprising. For one, the RTD space has continued to be one of the few bright spots in the industry, especially recently. According to the DISCUS economic briefing in February, sales of spirits-based RTDs are surging, with a rise of 16.4% to nearly $3.8 billion in 2025— “even as sales for most spirits categories pulled back in the same period,” says Wine Enthusiast writer-at-large Kara Newman, who reviews RTDs for the magazine. 

While Loverboy isn’t a spirits-based RTD—it is made with orange wine and agave wine, with a line of hard teas that “might be a malt bev or fortified with wine or something else,” says Newman, noting the category’s murkiness—the space is undeniably thriving. Smaller wine formats, like RTDs, are continuing to grow in popularity, according to recent WMC data. 

What’s more, unlike most brands, Loverboy has benefited from consistent free marketing for years to the millions of Bravo fans who tune into Summer House. According to Deadline, Season 10 of Summer House’s February 3 premiere racked up 2 million cross-platform viewers in seven days, marking the strongest debut in the show’s history. And this was long before the Amanda-West joint situationship press release. 

How could a brand with so much visibility, in a thriving category, face such financial hardship? And how could other small brands—with no free marketing, let alone viral love triangles—even possibly hope to compete in such an industry?

“The problem for a small brand in a crowded space is finding ways to inspire people to purchase your product at minimal marketing cost,” says Megan Klein, founder and CEO of the brand Little Saints, which sells non-alcoholic, mushroom-infused RTD cocktails and spirits. She says that the majority of the brand’s organic publicity came from her appearance on Shark Tank in 2024, which helped get retail buyers’ attention and boosted online sales. “But we found that celebrity buzz only gets you to that first purchase, and a brand will die unless it has a significant repeat customer base,” adds Klein.

Alas, such is the plight of most small businesses in most industries, and the beverage space is particularly cutthroat. By law in most states, the three-tier system dictates that you must sell your drink to a distributor before getting it into stores, and the distribution market is dominated by just a few big companies. Then there are the regulatory and legal hurdles, the massive upfront capital requirement, and inventory overhead.

Moreover, it is exceedingly difficult to stand out from the pack as a newer RTD brand. 

“After several years of strong growth in the RTD space, the market appears to be saturated, and we’re seeing some of the smaller players consolidating or exiting right now,” says Newman. 

Bravo superfan and Wine Enthusiast managing editor Jordon Kaplan also suspects Loverboy’s branding has been limiting. “Loverboy had the hurdle of entering into a very crowded RTD market, which is all very summer-oriented,” she says. “And while everyone should watch Summer House, marketing exclusively through Bravo was likely limiting exposure.”

Klein puts it this way: “Even if a small brand has great-tasting liquid and solid value propositions, if it can’t continue to market effectively, it’s going to get out-marketed by the bigger brands.”

Loverboy launched already well inside the RTD boom, and the biggest players are, well, extremely big. “The market felt like it had a million options, and White Claw and Twisted Tea, a little later, were king,” says Kaplan. (White Claw is owned by Mark Anthony Brands, the fourth largest brewing company in America, and Twisted Tea belongs to Boston Beer Company, which also owns Truly, Angry Orchard, and Samuel Adams.)

Can ‘Scamanda’ Save Loverboy?

By all accounts, the stuff tastes good. Newman hasn’t reviewed it, but Kaplan enjoyed the Limoncello. 

So what’s in store for Loverboy? “Scandoval,” the cheating scandal that emerged from Bravo’s Vanderpump Rules in 2023, might hold some clues. (If you live a healthy, peaceful life and are unfamiliar with what went down: Tom Sandoval cheated on his partner of nearly a decade, Ariana Maddox, with her friend Raquel/Rachel Leviss.)

“Ciara is very likely to get the Ariana treatment,” she says. “If Kyle is the James of this situation, he’s already much more likable, so if he can capitalize on the sympathy and news cycle with merch or new flavors or a limited run of something new, he might be able to pull himself up. And maybe a new lead graphic designer … it feels like there is an opening.”
